chunk_summary: This section discusses supplementary feeding regimens and safe product use for managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), emphasizing guidelines, nutritional products, and safety considerations.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of supplementary feeding in managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
Identify safe practices and products for supplementary feeding regimens.
Learn key nutritional guidelines for addressing MAM effectively.
key_facts: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) affects millions globally, particularly children under 5 years.
Supplementary feeding regimens often include ready-to-use therapeutic foods (RUTF) or fortified blended foods.
Safe product use requires adherence to hygiene standards and proper storage conditions.
WHO guidelines recommend energy-dense foods with 400-500 kcal per serving for MAM management.
Monitoring weight gain and nutritional status is critical during supplementary feeding interventions.
reference_quote: WHO recommends energy-dense supplementary foods with 400-500 kcal per serving to address moderate acute malnutrition effectively.
